title: Convolutional Neural Networks
blurb: ★★★ Core / must-know · Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) exploit the spatial structure of images through **weight sharing** and local **filters**, replacing the billions of parameters a fully connected layer would need. This chapter covers convolution and pooling, feature (activation) maps, spatial-size arithmetic, parameter counting, and building a CNN in code.

knowledge_points.title: ⭐ Mock Exam: Build/Extend a CNN + CNN Concepts
knowledge_points.recap: CNN is a guaranteed exam topic: one code question (extend a Keras CNN with Conv2D/Flatten/Dense/BatchNormalization then compile/fit/evaluate) and one concept+calculation question (conv vs FC, padding, output-size formula).

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to extend, compile and evaluate the CNNs (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
# (a) extend model1: one more Conv2D + a fully connected head
model1.add(Conv2D(num_convolutional_filters, (3, 3), activation='relu'))
model1.add(Flatten())
model1.add(Dense(64, activation='relu'))
model1.add(Dense(output_dimension, activation='sigmoid'))

# (b) extend model2: add BatchNormalization (needs no parameters)
model2.add(BatchNormalization())
model2.add(Conv2D(num_convolutional_filters, (3, 3), activation='relu'))
model2.add(Flatten())
model2.add(Dense(64, activation='relu'))
model2.add(Dense(output_dimension, activation='sigmoid'))

# (c) compile + fit both
for model in [model1, model2]:
    model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='binary_crossentropy')
    model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=10, batch_size=32,
              validation_data=(X_val, y_val))

# (d) evaluate + compare
loss1, acc1 = model1.evaluate(X_test, y_test)
loss2, acc2 = model2.evaluate(X_test, y_test)
print('better:', 'model1' if acc1 > acc2 else 'model2')
```

BatchNormalization takes no arguments. Use `binary_crossentropy` + `adam`; always pass `validation_data`; compare on the **test** set.
questions.extend: Grading points: extra Conv2D with activation, Flatten before Dense, BatchNormalization(), compile with loss+optimizer, fit with validation, evaluate on test.

variants.code:
```python
# (a) extend model1 with one more Conv2D + a Dense head
model1.add(____(1)(num_convolutional_filters, (3, 3), activation=____(2)))
model1.add(Flatten())
model1.add(Dense(64, activation='relu'))
model1.add(Dense(output_dimension, activation=____(3)))
```

```python
# (b) extend model2 with BatchNormalization + Dense head
model2.add(____(1)())            # normalize activations, no params
model2.add(Conv2D(num_convolutional_filters, (3, 3), activation='relu'))
model2.add(____(2)())            # 3-D feature maps -> 1-D vector
model2.add(Dense(64, activation='relu'))
model2.add(Dense(output_dimension, activation='sigmoid'))
```

```python
# (c)+(d) compile, fit, evaluate
model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss=____(1))
model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=10, batch_size=32,
          validation_data=(____(2), y_val))
loss, acc = model.____(3)(X_test, y_test)
```

questions.q:
(a) What benefits do convolutional layers offer over fully connected layers for images?
(b) Why is padding necessary when applying a strided filter?
(c) Given input **64×64×3**, applying **7 filters of size 6×6** with **stride 2** and **padding 2**, what is the output volume?
questions.answer:
**(a) Benefits:** **local connectivity** (filters see small receptive fields matching image structure); **parameter sharing** (one filter slides over the whole image → far fewer parameters, less overfitting); **translation invariance** (a feature is detected anywhere); **computational efficiency**.

**(b) Padding:** without it feature maps shrink after every conv (faster with stride > 1). Padding lets you **control output size** ('same' keeps H×W), stops **border pixels** being under-used, and prevents maps from vanishing after several layers.

**(c) Output size** uses $\left\lfloor\frac{W-F+2P}{S}\right\rfloor+1$ with $W=64, F=6, P=2, S=2$:

$$\frac{64-6+4}{2}+1=\frac{62}{2}+1=31+1=32.$$

Depth = number of filters = 7. **Output volume: 32 × 32 × 7.**

knowledge_points.title: From Fully Connected to Convolution: Filters & Feature Maps
knowledge_points.recap: A fully connected (FC) layer flattens an image (e.g. 32x32x3 -> 3072x1) and connects every pixel to every neuron, destroying spatial structure and exploding the parameter count (a 1000x1000x3 image into a 1000-neuron layer needs ~3 billion weights). The **convolution layer** fixes this with **weight sharing**: a small **filter** (e.g. 5x5x3) that always extends the *full depth* of the input is slid over the image, computing a dot product at each location. Each filter produces one 2D **activation map** (feature map). Stacking K filters gives an output volume of depth K (e.g. six 5x5x3 filters on a 32x32x3 input -> 28x28x6). A ConvNet stacks CONV+ReLU (+POOL) layers so early layers detect edges and later layers detect higher-level features.

questions.q: Why are convolution layers preferred over fully connected layers for image input?
questions.answer: They use **weight sharing** and local filters, which drastically reduces parameters and **preserves the spatial structure** of the image.

options.text: They preserve spatial structure and share weights, cutting parameters versus an FC layer
They always have more parameters, which increases accuracy
They flatten the image to a 1D vector before processing
They connect every input pixel to every output neuron

questions.q: What is an **activation map** (feature map), and how is it produced? What determines the depth of a convolution layer's output volume?
questions.answer: An **activation map** is the 2D output produced by **convolving one filter** over all spatial locations of the input: at each position the filter takes a dot product with a local patch (plus bias), yielding one number, and sliding it across the image builds the full map. A filter always spans the **full depth** of the input (e.g. a 5x5x3 filter on a 3-channel image is a 75-dimensional dot product). The output volume's **depth equals the number of filters K**: e.g. six 5x5x3 filters on a 32x32x3 image give a 28x28x6 volume.
questions.extend: This is why a ConvNet is described as a *sequence of convolution layers interspersed with activation functions* (e.g. 32x32x3 -> CONV/ReLU -> 28x28x6 -> CONV/ReLU -> 24x24x10).

questions.q: A 32x32x3 input is convolved with a single 5x5x3 filter (stride 1, no padding). How many parameters (weights) does that one filter use for a single dot product, ignoring bias?
questions.answer: A 5x5x3 filter has $5 \times 5 \times 3 = 75$ weights (a 75-dimensional dot product); adding a bias gives 76.

questions.q: Explain the difference between **translation equivariance** (convolution layers) and **translation invariance** (pooling layers) in one or two sentences each.
questions.answer: **Translation equivariance** (convolution): if the input shifts, the output feature map shifts by the same amount, $f(x(i-t)) = y(i-t)$ — the *response moves with the feature*. **Translation invariance** (pooling): a small shift of the input leaves the pooled output essentially unchanged, $f(x(i-t)) = y(i)$ — the layer becomes *insensitive to exact position*. Convolution detects *where* a feature is; pooling makes the representation robust to small translations.

knowledge_points.title: Output Spatial Size, Padding, Pooling & Parameter Counting
knowledge_points.recap: The spatial size of a convolution output is given by $\left(\frac{W - F + 2P}{S}\right) + 1$, where W is the input size, F the filter size, S the stride and P the zero-padding. To *preserve* spatial size with stride 1, use $P = (F-1)/2$ (F=3 -> P=1, F=5 -> P=2). The number of learnable parameters in a conv layer is $(F \cdot F \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot K$ (the +1 is the per-filter bias). **Pooling** (e.g. 2x2 max pooling, stride 2) downsamples each activation map independently, making the representation smaller, cheaper, and more translation-invariant; common settings are F=2/S=2 or F=3/S=2.

questions.q: Input volume 32x32x3 is passed through a conv layer of 10 filters of size 5x5, stride 1, pad 2. Compute (a) the output spatial width/height and (b) the total number of parameters in the layer.
questions.answer:
**(a) Output spatial size:** using $\left(\frac{W-F+2P}{S}\right)+1 = \frac{32-5+2\cdot2}{1}+1 = \frac{31}{1}+1 = 32$, so the output volume is **32x32x10** (depth = number of filters).

**(b) Parameters:** each filter has $5\cdot5\cdot3 + 1 = 76$ weights (+1 bias), and there are 10 filters, so $76 \times 10 = \mathbf{760}$ parameters.

questions.q: AlexNet CONV1: input 227x227x3, 96 filters of size 11x11, stride 4, no padding. Compute the output spatial size and the number of parameters in this layer.
questions.answer:
**Output spatial size:** $\frac{227-11}{4}+1 = \frac{216}{4}+1 = 54+1 = 55$, giving an output volume of **55x55x96**.

**Parameters:** $(11\cdot11\cdot3)\cdot96 = 363 \cdot 96 \approx 35\text{K}$ (about 34,848 without bias).

questions.q: You apply a 3x3 filter with stride 1 to a 7x7 input and want the output to stay 7x7. How much zero padding P is required?
questions.answer: $P = (F-1)/2 = (3-1)/2 = 1$. With one pixel of border padding, $\frac{7-3+2\cdot1}{1}+1 = 7$, so the spatial size is preserved.

questions.q: What does a max-pooling layer do in a CNN?
questions.answer: It **downsamples each activation map independently** (e.g. 2x2 max pool, stride 2 keeps the max of each 2x2 window), making the representation smaller and more manageable and adding a degree of **translation invariance**. It has no learnable parameters.

options.text: Downsamples each feature map independently, shrinking the representation and adding translation invariance
Adds trainable weights to increase model capacity
Connects every neuron to the full input volume
Increases the spatial resolution of the feature maps

questions.q: A 7x7 input is convolved with a 3x3 filter at stride 3 (no padding). Why does this configuration fail, and what does the formula predict?
questions.answer: The formula gives $\frac{7-3}{3}+1 = \frac{4}{3}+1 = 2.33$, which is **not an integer** — the filter does not fit evenly, so the 3x3 filter *cannot be applied* on a 7x7 input with stride 3. Valid stride/filter/padding combinations must yield an integer output size.

knowledge_points.title: Building a CNN in Code
knowledge_points.recap: A basic image classifier stacks convolution layers (each followed by a **ReLU** activation), then **flattens** the 3D feature volume into a vector and finishes with fully connected (Dense) layers, ending in a **softmax** for multi-class classification. **MaxPooling** shrinks feature maps and **Dropout** regularizes. In the lab this is built with Keras' `Sequential` API (the analogous PyTorch model subclasses `nn.Module` with `nn.Conv2d`, `nn.MaxPool2d` and a `forward` method). Note that Keras `Conv2D` expects channels-last input `(H, W, C)` e.g. `(28, 28, 1)` for MNIST.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to build the model (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
from keras.models import Sequential
from keras.layers import Conv2D, Dense, Activation, Flatten

model = Sequential()
# 1st Conv layer (needs input_shape on the first layer only)
model.add(Conv2D(filters=32, kernel_size=(3, 3),
                 input_shape=(28, 28, 1), activation='relu'))
# 2nd Conv layer
model.add(Conv2D(filters=32, kernel_size=(3, 3), activation='relu'))
# Flatten 3D feature maps -> 1D vector before Dense layers
model.add(Flatten())
model.add(Dense(units=128))
model.add(Activation('relu'))
# Prediction layer: 10 classes with softmax
model.add(Dense(units=10))
model.add(Activation('softmax'))

model.compile(loss='categorical_crossentropy',
              optimizer='adam', metrics=['accuracy'])
```
Key points: only the **first layer** declares `input_shape=(28, 28, 1)`; you must **Flatten** before Dense layers; the last layer uses **softmax** (10 units) with **categorical_crossentropy** for classification.
questions.extend:
The equivalent PyTorch model:
```python
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class VanillaCNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(1, 32, kernel_size=3)   # 28->26
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(32, 32, kernel_size=3)  # 26->24
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(32 * 24 * 24, 128)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(128, 10)
    def forward(self, x):
        x = F.relu(self.conv1(x))
        x = F.relu(self.conv2(x))
        x = x.flatten(1)
        x = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        return self.fc2(x)   # softmax handled by CrossEntropyLoss
```

variants.code:
```python
model = Sequential()
model.add(Conv2D(filters=32, kernel_size=(3, 3),
                 input_shape=____(1), activation=____(2)))
model.add(Conv2D(filters=32, kernel_size=(3, 3), activation='relu'))
```

```python
model.add(____(1)(filters=32, kernel_size=(3, 3), activation='relu'))  # 2nd conv
model.add(____(2)())            # before Dense layers
model.compile(loss=____(3),
              optimizer='adam', metrics=['accuracy'])
```

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to add pooling and dropout (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
Insert a **MaxPooling2D** layer *after the convolutional block* (to downsample the feature maps) and **Dropout** *after pooling* and again *after the Dense(128)* layer (to regularize):
```python
from keras.layers import MaxPooling2D, Dropout

model.add(Conv2D(32, (3, 3), input_shape=(28, 28, 1), activation='relu'))
model.add(Conv2D(32, (3, 3), activation='relu'))
model.add(MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2, 2)))   # downsample
model.add(Dropout(rate=0.25))               # regularize conv features
model.add(Flatten())
model.add(Dense(128))
model.add(Activation('relu'))
model.add(Dropout(rate=0.5))                 # regularize dense layer
model.add(Dense(10))
model.add(Activation('softmax'))
```
**Pooling** goes after the conv layers to make the representation smaller and translation-tolerant; **Dropout** goes after pooling and after the dense layer to reduce overfitting by randomly zeroing activations during training.

variants.code:

```python
model.add(Conv2D(32, (3, 3), input_shape=(28, 28, 1), activation='relu'))
model.add(Conv2D(32, (3, 3), activation='relu'))
model.add(____(1)(pool_size=(2, 2)))   # downsample
model.add(____(2)(rate=0.25))          # regularize conv features
model.add(Flatten())
```

```python
model.add(Flatten())
model.add(Dense(128))
model.add(Activation(____(1)))
model.add(Dropout(rate=____(2)))       # regularize dense layer
model.add(Dense(10))
model.add(Activation(____(3)))
```

```python
model.add(Conv2D(32, (____(1), 3), activation='relu'))
model.add(MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(____(2), 2)))
model.add(Dropout(____(3)))            # regularize conv features
```

questions.q: In the vanilla MNIST CNN, the input is 28x28x1 and both conv layers use 3x3 filters with stride 1 and no padding. What spatial size does the tensor have entering the Flatten layer, and how many features does Flatten produce?
questions.answer: Each 3x3 valid convolution reduces the spatial size by 2: $28 \to 26 \to 24$. So the tensor entering Flatten is **24x24x32**, and Flatten produces $24 \times 24 \times 32 = \mathbf{18432}$ features.

knowledge_points.title: ✍️ Code Fill-in-the-Blank
knowledge_points.recap: Reconstruct a CNN and the Mock-Q2 extension by typing the missing layers.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to build the model (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
from keras.models import Sequential
from keras.layers import Conv2D, MaxPooling2D, Flatten, Dense
model = Sequential()
model.add(Conv2D(32, (3,3), activation='relu', input_shape=(28,28,1)))
model.add(MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2,2)))
model.add(Flatten())
model.add(Dense(128, activation='relu'))
model.add(Dense(10, activation='softmax'))
```
questions.extend: Order: Conv2D → (Pool) → Flatten → Dense. Never forget Flatten before Dense.

variants.code:

```python
model.add(____(1)(32, (3,3), activation='relu', input_shape=(28,28,1)))
model.add(____(2)(pool_size=(2,2)))     # halve spatial size
model.add(____(3)())                     # 3-D feature maps -> 1-D vector
model.add(Dense(128, activation='relu'))
model.add(Dense(10, activation=____(4)))
```

```python
model.add(Conv2D(____(1), (____(2), 3), activation=____(3), input_shape=(28,28,1)))
model.add(MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2,2)))
model.add(Flatten())
model.add(Dense(128, activation='relu'))
```

knowledge_points.title: 🔮 SS26 Likely Exam Questions (predicted)
knowledge_points.recap: Predicted, exam-style questions in the professor's format — concept-first, plus a Keras code item where relevant. Not from a past paper; use to rehearse likely SS26 topics.

questions.q:
A convolution layer receives an input volume of **128×128×3** and applies **16 filters of size 5×5** with **stride 3** and **padding 1**.
(a) Compute the output volume (width, height and depth).
(b) How many **learnable parameters** does the layer have (include biases)?
questions.answer:
**(a)** Using $\left\lfloor\frac{W-F+2P}{S}\right\rfloor+1$ with $W=128,\ F=5,\ P=1,\ S=3$:
$$\left\lfloor\frac{128-5+2}{3}\right\rfloor+1=\left\lfloor\frac{125}{3}\right\rfloor+1=41+1=42.$$
Depth = number of filters = **16**, so the output volume is **42×42×16**.

**(b)** Each filter spans the full input depth: $5\times5\times3=75$ weights $+\,1$ bias $=76$. With 16 filters: $76\times16=\mathbf{1216}$ parameters.
questions.extend: Note the **floor**: $125/3=41.67$ rounds down to 41, so the output side is 42. Parameter count is independent of input spatial size thanks to **weight sharing**.

questions.q:
(a) Why is a **pooling** layer used in a CNN, and does it add trainable parameters?
(b) **Name one situation where you would use 'same' padding and one where you would NOT (or would use 'valid'),** and explain why.
(c) In one sentence, how does pooling differ from a strided convolution for downsampling?
questions.answer:
**(a)** **Pooling** (e.g. 2×2 max pool, stride 2) **downsamples each feature map independently**, shrinking the representation so it is cheaper to compute and store, and it grants a degree of **translation invariance** (small shifts of the input leave the pooled output nearly unchanged). It has **no learnable parameters**.

**(b)** *Would use 'same':* when stacking **many conv layers** and you want to **preserve spatial size** so feature maps do not shrink to nothing and **border pixels** are not under-used (with stride 1, $P=(F-1)/2$). *Would NOT use 'same' / use 'valid':* when you deliberately want to **reduce spatial size** (e.g. a downsampling/stride>1 stage) or when padding artificial zeros at the border would be undesirable.

**(c)** Pooling downsamples with a **fixed, non-learned** aggregation (max/avg), whereas a **strided convolution** downsamples with **learned** filters.
questions.extend: Max pooling keeps the strongest activation in each window; average pooling smooths. Common settings: F=2/S=2 or F=3/S=2.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to extend the CNN into a 10-class classifier (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
from keras.layers import MaxPooling2D, Flatten, Dense, Dropout

# model already ends in Conv2D(...) blocks producing 3-D feature maps
model.add(MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2, 2)))   # downsample
model.add(Flatten())                        # 3-D -> 1-D before Dense
model.add(Dense(128, activation='relu'))
model.add(Dropout(0.5))                     # regularize the dense head
model.add(Dense(10, activation='softmax'))  # 10 classes

model.compile(optimizer='adam',
              loss='categorical_crossentropy',
              metrics=['accuracy'])

model.fit(X_train, y_train,
          epochs=10, batch_size=64,
          validation_data=(X_val, y_val))

loss, acc = model.evaluate(X_test, y_test)  # test-set performance
print('test acc:', acc)
```
Grading points: **Flatten before Dense**, **activation on each layer**, **softmax(10) + categorical_crossentropy** for multi-class, **Dropout/pooling** for regularization/downsampling, **validation_data** in `fit`, **evaluate on the test set**.
questions.extend: If labels are integers (not one-hot), use `sparse_categorical_crossentropy`. For binary output use `Dense(1, 'sigmoid')` + `binary_crossentropy`.

```python
model.add(____(2)())                    # 3-D -> 1-D before Dense
model.add(Dense(128, activation='relu'))
model.add(Dropout(0.5))
model.add(Dense(10, activation=____(3)))  # 10 classes
```

```python
model.compile(optimizer=____(1),
              loss=____(2),
              metrics=['accuracy'])
model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=10, batch_size=64,
          validation_data=(X_val, y_val))
loss, acc = model.____(3)(X_test, y_test)
```
